title: Feather impression tile set
subtitle: Four square tiles with pressed feather relief
excerpt: A set of four 100 mm square air-dry clay tiles, each carrying the detailed impression of a different feather pressed into the soft surface. No kiln needed. PVA sealed for wall display or use as jewellery resting tiles.

glossaryTerms.definition: the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
unfired clay work at any stage from freshly formed to bone-dry

content.text: This set of four square tiles captures the barb structure of real feathers as a raised relief in air-dry clay. Each tile is 100 mm by 100 mm and 8 mm thick. The feathers are pressed into soft clay, peeled away, and the impression is bordered with a simple incised line. No kiln is required. The finished tiles are sealed with PVA and can be mounted as a wall grouping or used as flat resting surfaces for rings or pendants. The clay produces
greenware
that holds feather detail with accuracy because the clay is pressed while still fully soft.
Preparation

Collect four feathers with distinct structures: a large flight feather (pheasant or crow works well for a bold vein), a smaller body feather with soft barbs, a striped or patterned feather, and a downy or fluffy feather for contrast. Clean feathers thoroughly and let them dry flat. Damp or oily feathers will stick to the clay instead of peeling cleanly. Cut two 8 mm spacer guides from scrap card to roll the clay to an even thickness. Lightly dampen the wooden board so the clay does not stick, but do not wet it so much that the clay slides.
Construction

Break off approximately 125 g of clay for the first tile. Wedge it briefly by folding and pressing to remove air pockets, then flatten into a rough square. Place the 8 mm spacer cards on either side and roll with the pottery rolling pin until the clay is even across the full width of the spacers.

Cut a 100 mm by 100 mm square using the fettling knife and a straight edge. Keep the off-cuts : they can be re-wedged for the next tile. Smooth the top surface lightly with a damp pottery sponge, removing any rolling-pin marks without adding water to the point where the clay goes sticky.

Centre the feather on the tile face-down, quill tip pointing toward the middle. Press firmly and evenly across the full length of the feather with your fingertips, working outward from the central rachis (the main stem) to the barb tips. Press hard enough to feel slight resistance from the board beneath : light pressure leaves a shallow impression that loses definition when dry.

Peel the feather away in one slow, continuous movement, starting at the quill tip. Hold the clay down at the edges with the other hand as you lift. If the feather sticks, slide the needle tool underneath one barb edge to release it without dragging the clay.
Using the needle tool, incise a border line 8 mm in from each edge of the tile. Draw the line in a single continuous pass with light, even pressure. The border will be at the
leather-hard
stage to incise cleanly : if the clay is still too soft, let it firm for 20 minutes before adding the border.

Pierce a 4 mm hanging hole at the top centre of the tile, 12 mm down from the upper edge, using the needle tool. Rotate the tool gently to widen the hole without cracking the clay. Smooth the rim of the hole with a damp fingertip.
Repeat steps 1 to 6 for the remaining three tiles, using a different feather on each. Set all four tiles flat on the wooden board to dry.
Drying
Leave the tiles on the wooden board in a dry room at room temperature for 24 to 2 days. Do not move them until they have reached
bone-dry

: no cool patches, no colour variation between wetter and drier areas, and no flex when lifted at a corner. Drying unevenly : for example near a heat source on one side only : can cause warping. If the edges begin to curl, place a flat board on top and weight it lightly.
Finishing

When fully bone-dry, apply undiluted PVA to all surfaces with a clean brush, including the back and the sides. The PVA sinks into the clay and hardens it. Leave to dry clear, about 30 minutes. Apply a second coat for additional water resistance. The seal does not change the colour of the impression: the detail reads the same after sealing. Thread cord or picture wire through the hanging holes for wall display.
Care

Dust with a dry cloth. The PVA seal provides light water resistance but the tiles are not waterproof for prolonged wetting. Display indoors, away from direct sunlight, which yellows the PVA over time. If a tile chips at a corner, smooth the damage with fine-grade sandpaper and re-seal the area with a small amount of PVA.
Variations
Paint the relief with diluted acrylic wash before sealing. Brush a single colour over the impression and then wipe it from the raised areas. This gives a tonal effect.
Press two feathers on a single tile for a layered impression. Press the first feather and lift it. Then press a smaller feather at an angle across the first impression.
Make the tiles rectangular (150 mm by 75 mm) and display them in a vertical column rather than a square grouping.
Incise the species name below the impression using the needle tool while the clay is at leather-hard. This gives a naturalist field-study look.

rows.problem: Feather sticks to clay and tears on removal
rows.cause: Feather was damp or had residual oils on the surface
rows.fix: Clean and dry the feather thoroughly before use. Hold the clay flat while peeling. Use the needle tool to release stuck barbs gently.

rows.problem: Impression is too shallow : detail disappears when dry
rows.fix: Re-roll the clay and press again, applying steady firm pressure across the whole feather length and working from the central rachis outward.

rows.cause: Uneven drying or a thin weak point in the slab
rows.fix: Dry slowly away from heat sources. Place a flat board on top during drying to prevent warping. Hairline cracks can be filled with a paste of clay slip and water once dry.

rows.problem: Border line is ragged or uneven
rows.cause: Clay too soft when incised, or too much pressure on the needle tool
rows.fix: Wait until leather-hard before incising. Draw the border line in one smooth pass without stopping. Use a ruler or straight edge as a guide.

rows.problem: PVA dries cloudy over the impression
rows.cause: PVA applied too thickly or clay not fully bone-dry
rows.fix: Ensure complete bone-dry before sealing. Apply PVA in thin coats and allow each to dry clear before adding the next.
